query: vedolizumab concentration in human breast milk

retrieved_docs.title: Infliximab-abda -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding considerations: Infliximab is typically either absent or present at only trace levels in human milk, with negligible oral bioavailability in nursing infants. Clinical follow-ups of infants exposed in utero and via breastfeeding have demonstrated normal development and no reported adverse effects. While minimal concentrations identified in some samples raise theoretical concerns, levels are insufficient to suggest risk of systemic immunosuppression. In mothers with inflammatory bowel disease, anti–tumor necrosis factor therapy has been associated with reduced concentrations of TNF and IP-10 in early postpartum milk. Continued postpartum use in women treated during pregnancy does not appear to extend infliximab clearance in the infant. Consensus supports the use of infliximab during lactation. Postponing therapy until 2 weeks after delivery may further reduce infant exposure. [47] AAD-NPF suggests that infants may be immunocompromised for 1 to 3 months after birth. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Omalizumab -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding considerations: Primary evidence suggests that the concentration of omalizumab in maternal milk is low. As omalizumab is a large protein molecule, the amount present in milk is likely to be very low. Moreover, omalizumab is expected to be destroyed in the infant's gastrointestinal tract, leading to minimal absorption. Therefore, omalizumab is considered a permissible drug for use during breastfeeding. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Emicizumab -- Administration -- Use in Specific Patient Population
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding Considerations: No data is available on the clinical use of emicizumab during breastfeeding. Emicizumab is a large protein molecule with high molecular weight; hence the concentration in milk is probably low. Hypothetically absorption of emicizumab is unlikely because it is probably destroyed in the infant's gastrointestinal tract. [18] According to product labeling, clinicians should consider the health benefits and risks of exposure to emicizumab in breastfeeding infants and the maternal need for emicizumab.

retrieved_docs.title: Evinacumab -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding considerations: There is no known risk for breastfeeding in these patients. Due to the high molecular weight of 146,000 Da, the presence of evinacumab in breast milk is expected to be minimal. Furthermore, it is anticipated that the protein may undergo degradation in the infant's gastrointestinal tract, resulting in limited absorption. Without further information, cautious use of evinacumab is advised during breastfeeding, particularly when nursing a neonate or preterm infant. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Infliximab -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding considerations: Infliximab is generally not detectable or found at very low levels in breast milk, with minimal absorption by the nursing infant. Studies on infants exposed to the drug in utero and breastfed during maternal infliximab therapy have shown no adverse effects and normal development. Although some women have minimal concentrations in their milk, suggesting potential local immune suppression in the gastrointestinal tract, these levels do not pose concerns about systemic immunosuppression. For mothers who used infliximab during pregnancy, continuing its use while breastfeeding does not prolong the drug's elimination by the infant. Expert consensus and professional guidelines affirm that infliximab has a low risk to the nursing infant and is considered acceptable for use during breastfeeding. To minimize transfer to the infant, it is recommended to wait at least 2 weeks postpartum before resuming therapy. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Anifrolumab  -- Administration -- Specific Patient Population
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding women: No information is available on the clinical use of anifrolumab during breastfeeding. As anifrolumab is a large protein molecule, the amount in milk is likely to be very low. Anifroluman may be destroyed in the infant's gastrointestinal tract and is probably minimally absorbed. [21] Pediatric patients: No dose adjustment information is available for pediatric patients as no safety and efficacy were evaluated for patients younger than 18.

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding considerations: Current data suggest that golimumab is present in breast milk at minimal levels. This drug is likely to undergo partial breakdown in the infant's gastrointestinal tract, resulting in minimal absorption. Given the limited data, caution is advised when administering golimumab to a woman who is breastfeeding, particularly if the infant is newborn or preterm. The British Society for Rheumatology guidelines suggest that golimumab may be compatible with breastfeeding. [15] Holding golimumab for at least 2 weeks postpartum may significantly reduce the amount of medication transferred to the nursing infant. [16] Pediatric patients: Golimumab is indicated for active psoriatic arthritis and polyarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis in patients aged 2 or older.

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor Necrosis Factor Inhibitors -- Adverse Effects -- Pregnancy and Lactation
retrieved_docs.content: Similarly, there is no data regarding the use of anti-TNF agents in lactating females. Anti-TNF agents have been found in the human milk at less than 1% of the maternal serum concentration, and there are no reports of adverse effects of anti-TNF agents in breastfed infants or on milk production. It is not known if the infant absorbs the anti-TNF agents at these low concentrations in human milk or not. Until more data is available, clinicians should avoid anti-TNF agents in lactating females.

retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Administration -- Breastfeeding women
retrieved_docs.content: Tocilizumab can be used while breastfeeding; no human data exists, but based on drug characteristics, there is no risk of infant harm. There is no human data regarding the effects on milk production.

retrieved_docs.title: Adalimumab -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding considerations: Limited data suggest that when mothers receive adalimumab injections, the presence of the medication in breast milk is minimal. Adalimumab, as a large protein molecule, is expected to undergo partial degradation in the infant's digestive system, resulting in minimal absorption. In some cases, breastfed infants did not exhibit detectable levels of adalimumab in their serum, and the available data indicate that adalimumab does not have adverse effects on nursing infants. Numerous experts and professional guidelines endorse the safe use of adalimumab during breastfeeding. To further minimize the potential for medication transfer to the infant, it may be advisable to wait at least 2 weeks after childbirth before resuming therapy. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Ixekizumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Ixekizumab should be used cautiously during breastfeeding, especially with newborns and preterm infants, because of limited clinical data. [7] Holding ixekizumab therapy for at least 2 weeks postpartum may minimize exposure to the baby. Ixekizumab is a large protein (146 KDa). The amount distributed to the breast milk is likely to be low, and the amount absorbed by the baby’s GI tract is also expected to be low, with some degradation of the protein in the GI tract.

retrieved_docs.title: Bamlanivimab -- Toxicity -- Specific Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Lactating mothers: Bamlanivimab has a molecular weight of 146,000 Da. Because it is a very large protein, the amount of the drug in the milk is expected to be low. [11] There is no data available regarding the effect of bamlanivimab on breast milk production and breastfed infants [2] . The use of the drug should be considered on an individual basis.

retrieved_docs.title: Aflibercept -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding considerations: Aflibercept is not recommended during breastfeeding according to the FDA-approved product labeling. The benefits of breastfeeding should be balanced against the mother’s clinical need for aflibercept and the potential risks to the breastfed infant. Literature reviews indicate that aflibercept, a 115-kDa protein, is expected to have negligible oral bioavailability and minimal presence in breast milk following IVT injection. However, VEGF in human milk contributes to gastrointestinal tract maturation in infants. Although systemic exposure of nursing infants to aflibercept is likely extremely low, its anti-VEGF activity presents a theoretical concern for potential interference with gut development. [33] Pediatric patients: The safety and efficacy of aflibercept have been documented in preterm infants with ROP. [34] [35] Older patients: Clinical trials have shown no significant differences in efficacy or safety of aflibercept related to advancing age.

retrieved_docs.title: Brodalumab -- Contraindications -- Children and Pregnancy
retrieved_docs.content: Though no contraindication exists, researchers have not investigated the efficacy and safety of brodalumab as a potential treatment in children or pregnant women with psoriasis or breastfed infants of mothers taking brodalumab. However, monoclonal antibodies are generally known to actively be transported through the placenta, especially in the third trimester, and get excreted in modest amounts in breast milk. Risks and benefits to the mother require assessment before initiation or discontinuation of brodalumab.

retrieved_docs.title: Romosozumab -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding considerations: Romosozumab is not approved for women of reproductive potential. In animal studies with pregnant rats, romosozumab administration beginning 6 weeks before mating and lactation resulted in detectable serum levels in offspring, confirming dose-dependent exposure during gestation and lactation. Pediatric patients: The safety and efficacy of romosozumab have not been established in pediatric patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Secukinumab -- Administration -- Specific Patient Population
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding Considerations: There is a lack of information concerning the clinical use of secukinumab during breastfeeding. As secukinumab has a high molecular weight and is a big protein molecule, the concentration in maternal milk is likely to be very low. Secukinumab is also probably partially destroyed in the infant's gastrointestinal tract. However, significant research is still needed. Hence, clinicians should be cautious while prescribing secukinumab during breastfeeding. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1206
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial sepsis of the newborn (unspecified), Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome, neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation (including the specific form caused by delayed development of the conjugating system), نزيف حديثي الولادة (neonatal hemorrhage), and يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice) are all conditions that occur during the neonatal period. The qualifier “Neonatal” therefore applies to each of these entities, indicating that their onset, presentation, or occurrence is confined to the first weeks of life. Recognizing that these diverse disorders share the common temporal context of the neonatal stage helps clinicians focus diagnostic evaluation and management strategies appropriate for newborn patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 1163
retrieved_docs.content: The qualifier “Neonatal (qualifier value)” designates conditions that manifest during the newborn period. It is applied to the occurrence of نزف سري بعد الولادة (umbilical bleeding after birth), نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract bleeding), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), يرقان نووي (physiologic neonatal jaundice), and يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (physiologic jaundice resulting from immature hepatic conjugation in fetuses or newborns). Recognizing these entities as neonatal helps clinicians focus assessment and management on disorders specific to the early post‑natal timeframe.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2140
retrieved_docs.content: Bacteriële sepsis van pasgeborene, niet gespecificeerd is a neonatal condition that can occur in newborns. The neonatal (qualifier value) is associated with several neonatal disorders, including نزف سري بعد الولادة (post‑umbilical bleeding after birth), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), يرقان نووي (physiologic jaundice), يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (physiologic jaundice caused by immature bilirubin conjugation in fetuses or newborns), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (prematurity‑related jaundice). These interrelated conditions illustrate the spectrum of neonatal complications that may present in the early post‑natal period, ranging from infectious sepsis to various forms of jaundice, and underscore the importance of recognizing neonatal qualifiers in clinical assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 0 -- Community 18
retrieved_docs.content: neonatal period, anchors a spectrum of early‑life disorders such as hereditary metabolic disease (Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome), multiple jaundice subtypes (neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation of bilirubin, breast‑milk jaundice, physiologic neonatal jaundice, hemolytic jaundice, perinatal jaundice, Rh‑alloimmunization‑related jaundice, prematurity‑associated jaundice), and hemorrhagic presentations (general neonatal hemorrhage, intracranial neonatal hemorrhage, umbilical bleeding after birth, respiratory‑tract bleeding). These hemorrhagic events are linked to an unspecified perinatal condition that may arise from perinatal infections, which include unspecified bacterial sepsis of the newborn and neonatal botulism. Laboratory findings described as “Below reference range” are interpreted in the context of hemolytic and perinatal jaundice, providing quantitative guidance for diagnosis. The disorder “Disorder relating to short gestation AND/OR low birthweight” is both a child of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2323
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial sepsis of the newborn (unspecified) is a neonatal condition that, together with a range of other neonatal disorders, defines the health challenges encountered during the neonatal period. Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation, postpartum hemorrhage (نزف سري بعد الولادة), neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ), breast‑milk jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي), and jaundice associated with preterm birth (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان) all occur within the neonatal timeframe. Recognizing that these entities—bacterial sepsis of the newborn, delayed‑conjugation jaundice, postpartum hemorrhage, respiratory tract hemorrhage, breast‑milk‑induced jaundice, and prematurity‑related jaundice—are interrelated manifestations of neonatal physiology and pathology is essential for accurate diagnosis, timely intervention, and optimal neonatal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1200
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn is linked to a spectrum of neonatal jaundice conditions and related complications. It is associated with neonatal jaundice caused by delayed conjugation, as well as breast‑milk jaundice, physiologic (newborn) jaundice, and jaundice that is specifically related to preterm birth. In addition, this condition can be accompanied by a loose umbilical cord clamp that leads to bleeding. Recognizing these interconnected manifestations is important for comprehensive assessment and management of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2268
retrieved_docs.content: The neonatal qualifier value encompasses several newborn conditions. It includes umbilical bleeding after birth, which can occur when a loose umbilical cord clamp leads to bleeding. It also occurs in various forms of neonatal jaundice, such as breast‑milk jaundice, physiologic jaundice that results from immature liver function in fetuses or newborns, and jaundice that is linked to premature delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 936
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal (qualifier value) refers to a group of conditions that manifest during the newborn period, including neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation, breast‑milk‑induced jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي), bleeding associated with a loose umbilical cord clamp (losgeraakte navelstrengklem; bloeding), post‑delivery umbilical hemorrhage (نزف سري بعد الولادة), and bleeding of the neonatal respiratory tract (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ). All of these entities are recognized as neonatal‑specific events, highlighting the range of metabolic and hemorrhagic complications that can occur in the early life of an infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1138
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is classified as a neonatal condition, indicating that it manifests during the newborn period. The neonatal (qualifier value) stage is also the context in which several specific clinical entities occur, including نزف سري بعد الولادة (umbilical bleeding after birth), نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract bleeding), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (premature‑related neonatal jaundice). Recognizing that these entities all occur in the neonatal timeframe helps clinicians understand the spectrum of disorders and presentations that may be present in newborns, including those affected by Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome, and guides appropriate evaluation and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 917
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is classified under neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn. This broader category encompasses several specific neonatal conditions, including نزف سري بعد الولادة (umbilical hemorrhage after birth), نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (respiratory tract hemorrhage in the newborn), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), and يرقان نووي (physiologic neonatal jaundice). Together, these entities represent key hemorrhagic and hyperbilirubinemic presentations that are important for the diagnosis, monitoring, and management of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 869
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal (qualifier value) serves as a broad category that includes several distinct neonatal conditions. Within this category, neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation is recognized as a specific form of jaundice that manifests in the newborn period. Additional conditions that occur in the neonatal timeframe include نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), يرقان نووي (physiologic or nuclear jaundice), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (prematurity‑related neonatal jaundice). Together, these entities illustrate the variety of etiologies—ranging from delayed bilirubin conjugation and breast‑milk factors to prematurity and respiratory bleeding—that can affect newborns, underscoring the importance of recognizing each specific presentation for accurate diagnosis and appropriate neonatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2324
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial sepsis of the newborn, unspecified, is classified as a neonatal condition. The qualifier “Neonatal (qualifier value)” is used to denote a range of disorders that occur in the newborn period, including several distinct types of neonatal jaundice: neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory distress), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (jaundice resulting from immature hepatic conjugation in fetuses or newborns), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (prematurity‑associated jaundice). These interrelated neonatal entities share the common context of early‑life physiology and pathology, underscoring the importance of recognizing their overlapping presentations for accurate diagnosis and appropriate management in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2892
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier is recognized as an appropriate therapeutic option for managing diabetes during pregnancy, acute cholestasis of the liver, and fetal distress syndrome, indicating its relevance across these distinct obstetric and hepatic conditions and its role in guiding patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2423
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial sepsis of the newborn, unspecified, is identified as a neonatal occurrence, indicating that this infectious condition manifests during the newborn period. Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is likewise described with a neonatal qualifier, signifying that the syndrome occurs in neonates. The neonatal period is also the context for several other specific conditions: neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ), breast‑milk jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي), physiologic neonatal jaundice (يرقان نووي), and jaundice associated with preterm birth (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان). Together, these relationships emphasize that bacterial sepsis, Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome, respiratory hemorrhage, and various forms of neonatal jaundice are all conditions that arise in the neonatal stage, underscoring the importance of recognizing and managing these disorders promptly in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1080
retrieved_docs.content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome is a disorder that manifests during the neonatal period. Several other conditions are also characteristic of this early life stage, including neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation, the bleeding complication associated with a loose umbilical cord clamp (losgeraakte navelstrengklem; bloeding), post‑natal umbilical bleeding (نزف سري بعد الولادة), and breast‑milk jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي). All of these entities are linked by their occurrence in the neonatal timeframe, highlighting the range of metabolic, hematologic, and umbilical‑related problems that can arise in newborns and underscoring the importance of vigilant assessment and management during this critical period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 1077
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn is linked to several related neonatal conditions and a bleeding complication. It encompasses neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation, reflecting immature hepatic conjugation capacity. It also includes bleeding that can arise from a loose umbilical cord clamp (losgeraakte navelstrengklem; bloeding). In addition, breast‑milk jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي) may be observed, as well as physiologic jaundice of the newborn caused by immature conjugation in the fetus or newborn (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد). Prematurity‑related jaundice (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان) is another manifestation associated with this condition. Recognizing these interconnected manifestations is essential for comprehensive assessment and management of affected newborns.
